entries.title: Война... но Биткойн НЕ крашва ?
entries.concept: The market at the moment is in absolute chaos.
entries.summary:
The market at the moment is in absolute chaos.
After the big movements of the first few weeks, the market just spread.
In the last two weeks, we saw a clean consolidation of Tesla and Range in a wide range.
And this is coincidentally, historically, exactly after such a period of chaos and a lot of movement, the biggest movements are usually taking place.
Because in these zones, big participants accumulate positions.
In today's video, we will look at the structure of Bitcoin, the key levels, the dominations, and we will see what are the real scenarios for this segment.
And so friends, before we start, I want to show you what happened with Bitcoin.
After me, we started with the Daily Time Frame.
I saw a lot of people from the 7th century being quite euphoric and expecting the market to not spread.
We went back to Range again.

entries.title: OWASP's Top 10 Ways to Attack LLMs: AI Vulnerabilities Exposed
entries.concept: You know what's catching a lot of teams off guard right now?
entries.summary:
You know what's catching a lot of teams off guard right now?
How easy it is for an LLM to leak something that it shouldn't, or be steered into doing something you never intended.
One clever prompt, one exposed training file, one sketchy plug-in, and suddenly your helpful AI assistant becomes a security incident just waiting to happen.
That's why the new OWASP Top 10 for AI Large Language Models, LLMs for short, really matters.
It cuts to the most common threats people are running into when they deploy these models in the real world.
And if you're new to OWASP, they're a global nonprofit focused on practical community-built security guidance.
They're the folks behind the classic Top 10 for Web Apps, and now they're doing the same for AI.
You can find all their work at owasp.org.
They came out with a Top 10 for Large Language Models in 2023, but a few years on, we've learned a lot, and that learning is reflected in an updated Top 10 list.
All right, let's break down what you need to watch out for when you put LLMs into production so you don't become the next victim.

entries.title: OpenClaw v2026 3 1 — Claude 4 6 & WebSocket Streaming
entries.concept: OpenCloud just shipped version 2026.3.1 and it is running Claude 4.6 with realtime websocket streaming and the agent response speed has completely changed the game.
entries.summary:
OpenCloud just shipped version 2026.3.1 and it is running Claude 4.6 with realtime websocket streaming and the agent response speed has completely changed the game.
The latest OpenClaw release is the most significant update since the framework's launch integrating OpenAI websocket streaming alongside Claude 4.6's adaptive reasoning engine to deliver near instantaneous agent decision-making.
Previously, OpenClaw communicated with LLMS via standard REST API polling, which introduced significant roundtrip latency between each agentic step.
Version 2026.3.1 replaces this entirely with persistent websocket connections, meaning the agent now receives a continuous token stream while simultaneously executing shell commands in parallel.
Combined with Claude 4.6's Six's adaptive reasoning, which dynamically adjusts its internal chain of thought depth based on task complexity.
OpenClaw can now handle multi-step developer workflows like diagnosing a server error, pulling the relevant logs, and deploying a hot fix in a single fluid, low latency execution loop.
The update also ships native Kubernetes support, making it production deployable at scale for the first time.
Realtime streaming plus adaptive reasoning is the architecture that turns a toy agent into genuine infrastructure.

entries.title: OpenClaw v2026 3 2 Just Dropped — 150 Fixes and a Built In PDF Brain
entries.concept: Open Claw just dropped version 2026.3.2 on March 3rd and the headline is 150 fixes and a built-in PDF brain.
entries.summary:
Open Claw just dropped version 2026.3.2 on March 3rd and the headline is 150 fixes and a built-in PDF brain.
But if you read the change log like that and move on, you're missing the actual story.
This release isn't a cleanup patch.
It's open claw systematically closing the gaps that were preventing developers from trusting it with real production workflows.
And the PDF engine is only the most visible piece of a much larger architectural shift.
First, native PDF analysis backed by Anthropic and Google as processing backends.
Second, a fully matured secret system that now covers 64 credential targets.
Third, security patches that close three separate privilege escalation vulnerability classes simultaneously.
Together, these changes move OpenClaw from a powerful personal tool that required constant babysitting into something that can run unsupervised on serious workloads without making you nervous.
Let's start with the headline feature because the implementation detail matters more than the feature name suggests.
